texto: Art is a conversation we are all invited to and are all worthy to participate in. Yes, great works can be intimidating, but no one else in the world has what you have—your voice, your eyes, your feeling and perspective. Other people have written great books, but no one else will ever write YOUR book. It's worth writing. That is the belief that carries me through.

texto: For the merest moment I couldn't breathe. Something inside me quivered, some oud string plucked by his words, and if I breathed it would stop.He did not know the truth of me, yet he had perceived something true about me that no one else had ever noticed. And in spite of that—or perhaps because of it—he believed me good, believed me worth taking seriously, and his belief, for one vertiginous moment, made me want to be better than I was.

texto: Camba had bent her long neck down to Ingar's level and was muttering in his ear. "Do you feel the breeze on your face?" I heard her say. "That's yours, and worth feeling. Look at those orange clouds. All the trials of a day may be endured if you know there's such a sky at the end of it. Some days I told my heart to wait, just wait, because the sunset would teach me again that my pain was nothing compared with the eternal, circling sky.

texto: Oh, you humans may prefer empathy and mercy, but that's like intuiting the answer to an equation: you still have to go back and work the problem to be certain you were right. We can come to genuinely moral conclusions by our own paths.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Rachel Hartman: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a trabalho. O trecho “Oh, you humans may prefer empathy and mercy, but that's like intuiting the answer to an equation:…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que trabalho, ethics, logic pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de trabalho e logic.

texto: Let the one who seeks justice be just.
explicacao: Citação de Rachel Hartman sobre temas ligados a reflexao. “Let the one who seeks justice be just.” articula reflexao, justice, seraphina em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como seraphina.

texto: I was just chased through St. Willibald’s, and you know why? Because I was kind to a quig. I scrupulously hide every legitimate reason for people to hate me, and then it turns out they don’t need legitimate reasons. Heaven has fashioned a knife of irony to stab me with.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Rachel Hartman que provoca temas ligados a reflexao. Ao questionar “I was just chased through St. Willibald’s, and you know why? Because I was kind to a quig. I scru…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, hatred, irony, com foco em irony.

texto: Was it probably true that reasoning beings were equal? It seemed more like a belief than a fact, even if I agreed with it. If you followed logic all the way back to its origin, did you inevitably end up at point of illogic, an article of faith?
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Rachel Hartman: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a fe. O trecho “Was it probably true that reasoning beings were equal? It seemed more like a belief than a fact, …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que fe, faith, logic pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de termino e logic.
